Shoaib Khan and Waris Khan S/o Noor Rehman have preferrecl this Criminal Jail Appeal against the impugned iudgment clated 2-1.06.?016 passed by the learned Specral Court-ll (C'NS) Karachi in Special Case No.192 of 2012, F.l.R. No 14 o( 2013 U /s 9-C' C N S Act' 1997 ' P S' ANF Clifton, Karachi whereby the appellants have been convicted for offence under section 6 punishable under section 9-C CNS Act' 1997 and sentenced to Life Imprisonment anrl frne of Rs.500,000/- (Rupees five hundred thousand only) each. In case of default in payment of fine they were ordered to suffer further Rigorous Imprisonment for tfuee years Benefit of section 382-8 Cr'P C' has also been extended to the aPPellants, 2. The brief iacts of the case as per FIR are that comPlainant Mohammacl Afzal of PS ANF Clifton, Karachi lociged FIR on 04 03'2013 at about 0145 hours and alleged therein that on 03.03.2013 he was present at PS and received spy information thrcugh high ups that international narcotic smugglers namely (i) Javed Khan, (ii) Alam Zaib, (iil) Zahid Khan anc{ (iv) Waris Khan were present along with huge quantity of narcotic contraband at Rafique Manzil' near Noor Masjid, Jubilee Cinema, Karachi and woultl smuggle narcotic by concealing it iu onions and if immediate action be taken recovery must be possible' Therefore' ou ?

The prosecution to Prove the charge examined 04 PW's who exhibiter'i various documents and other items in support of the prosecution case where after the prosecution closed its side. Tlre statements under section 342 Cr'l'C of the accused were recorded in which they cleniecl the alleSations leveled against them anrl claimed false implication. All tlre accused persons cxarniuecl themselves uncler oath ancl again claimed false implication They caller'l 2 DW's in support oI their clefense of false implication. 5. Learnerl Special Court-ll (C.N.S.) Karachi after hearing the learnecl counsel for the parties and assessment of evidence available on recorcl, vitle juclgment dated 21.06.2016, convicted and sentenced the apPellants as statccl above, hence tl-rese appeals has been filed against their convictions' 6. The facts of the case as well as evidence produced before the trial court finrl an elabolate mention in tl're impugneel lucigment, therefore, the same are not reproducecl here so as to avoitl duplication at-tc1 unnecessarY repetition' 7. Learned counsel for the appellants has contended that the appellants are innocent and have been falsely implicated in the case, that the heroin was foisted on the appellants, that no heroin powcier was actually recovered, that weiSht of the recovered hereon was in doubt, that there was no evidence of safe custody ancl as such for any of the above reasons the appellants be acquitted by 2./ A t18 lil I extencling to thern the benefit of the doubt. In support of his contentions he placed reliance on Ikramullah V The State (2015 SCMR 1002) ancl Syed Karim V ANF (PLD 2003 Kar 606) 8. On the other hancl Learnetl Special Prosecutor ANF has fully supported the impugned jurigment ancl has contencled that the accused had been caught red hamlecl in a flat which they hacl ret.rted, that there was no doubt as to the weiglrt of the recoveretl l-reroin powder as proved by the chemical report which was positive and as such the prosecution hatl proved its case beyond a reasonable doubt and the appeals should be dismissed. 9.

In support of his contentions he placed reliance on Ikramullah V The State (2015 SCMR 1002) ancl Syed Karim V ANF (PLD 2003 Kar 606) 8. On the other hancl Learnetl Special Prosecutor ANF has fully supported the impugned jurigment ancl has contencled that the accused had been caught red hamlecl in a flat which they hacl ret.rted, that there was no doubt as to the weiglrt of the recoveretl l-reroin powder as proved by the chemical report which was positive and as such the prosecution hatl proved its case beyond a reasonable doubt and the appeals should be dismissed. 9. We have hearcl the arguments of the learned counsel for the Parties, gone through the entire eviclence which has been reacl out by the learned counsel for the appellants, the impugnecl juc{gment with thelr able assistance antl have consiclered the relevant Iaw. 10. ln our view after our reassessment of the evidence we are of the view that the prosecution has proved its case beyonci a reasonable doubt against tl.re appellants for the following reasons:- (a) That the FIR was registered with promptitude giving no tirne for concoction keeping in view that as Per the prosecution evidence it took about eight to nine hours to count and weigh the large amount of capsules ancl couttt the onions and the S.161 statements were recorclerl promptly u'hich were not significantly improved upou bv any PW at the time of giving evitlence. (b) That the arrest ancl recovery was made on the sPot in a flat on the pointation of a spy informer and the appellants were caught reti harrc{ecl with the narcotics by the police/ANF whose evidence fully corroborates each other in all material respects as well as the prosecution case. lt is it u'ell settled by now that the eviclence of a police/ANF witness is as reliable as any other witness provieled that no enmity exists between thetn anci the accused anr'l in this case the police/ANF PW's have no enmity with the appellants anr'l the appellants have ar.tnitted in their evitlence that the police/ ANF had no enmity with therr aucl in fact wcre disintercsted witnesses, Y ^.,1 oAb,| who had no reason to falsely implicate the appellants ln this respect reliance is placed on Iiaz Ahmed V The State (2009 SCMR ee). (c) That the flat had been rented by the appellants in which the arrest ancl recovery was made ancl according to PW 3 Muharnmecl Iqbal who l-rad rented the flat to one o[ the appellants (which belonged to PW 4 Ali Raj) the appellants had told him that thev wanted to use a patt of the flat as a go down for storing onions and potatoes antl a few clays before the raid he saw onions bein2i tlroppecl at the flats wlrich he l-rad rented where one of the appellants was Present.

On the other hancl Learnetl Special Prosecutor ANF has fully supported the impugned jurigment ancl has contencled that the accused had been caught red hamlecl in a flat which they hacl ret.rted, that there was no doubt as to the weiglrt of the recoveretl l-reroin powder as proved by the chemical report which was positive and as such the prosecution hatl proved its case beyond a reasonable doubt and the appeals should be dismissed. 9. We have hearcl the arguments of the learned counsel for the Parties, gone through the entire eviclence which has been reacl out by the learned counsel for the appellants, the impugnecl juc{gment with thelr able assistance antl have consiclered the relevant Iaw. 10. ln our view after our reassessment of the evidence we are of the view that the prosecution has proved its case beyonci a reasonable doubt against tl.re appellants for the following reasons:- (a) That the FIR was registered with promptitude giving no tirne for concoction keeping in view that as Per the prosecution evidence it took about eight to nine hours to count and weigh the large amount of capsules ancl couttt the onions and the S.161 statements were recorclerl promptly u'hich were not significantly improved upou bv any PW at the time of giving evitlence. (b) That the arrest ancl recovery was made on the sPot in a flat on the pointation of a spy informer and the appellants were caught reti harrc{ecl with the narcotics by the police/ANF whose evidence fully corroborates each other in all material respects as well as the prosecution case. lt is it u'ell settled by now that the eviclence of a police/ANF witness is as reliable as any other witness provieled that no enmity exists between thetn anci the accused anr'l in this case the police/ANF PW's have no enmity with the appellants anr'l the appellants have ar.tnitted in their evitlence that the police/ ANF had no enmity with therr aucl in fact wcre disintercsted witnesses, Y ^.,1 oAb,| who had no reason to falsely implicate the appellants ln this respect reliance is placed on Iiaz Ahmed V The State (2009 SCMR ee). (c) That the flat had been rented by the appellants in which the arrest ancl recovery was made ancl according to PW 3 Muharnmecl Iqbal who l-rad rented the flat to one o[ the appellants (which belonged to PW 4 Ali Raj) the appellants had told him that thev wanted to use a patt of the flat as a go down for storing onions and potatoes antl a few clays before the raid he saw onions bein2i tlroppecl at the flats wlrich he l-rad rented where one of the appellants was Present. The lease agreement was exhibited at trial Neither PW's 3 ancl 4 who were involved in the leasing of the flat hacl any enmity witl.r the appellants anc'l had no reason to falsely implicate then in this case (ci) That there are no major contradictions in the evidence of tl.re PW's ancl it is rvell settled by now that minor contradictions which clo not effect the materiality of the evidence can be ignorecl ln this resPect reliance is placed on Zakir Khan V State (i995 SCMR 1793)' (e) That we clo not fintl any cleficiencies in the weighing of the narcotics ancl that the correct weight has been recorded as per memo of recovery antl chemical report' (f) That the recovered narcotics were kePt in safe custocly at thc Malkana and were sent for chemical analysis without any clelav ancl such chemical rePort was Positive and compliecl with all relevant legal requirements. (g) 'Ihat is extremely clifficult for such a large amount of heroin to be foisted on the appellants which is usually not readily available with the police/ANF and if the police/ANF had wanted to falsely implicate the appellants they would not have wasted their time making up an ingenious story of the narcotic capsules being hidden in onions and even recovering 1,000 KG of onions, glue and cuttil.tg equipment for the purpose from the flat. They would l.rave simply 7 \ 5

The lease agreement was exhibited at trial Neither PW's 3 ancl 4 who were involved in the leasing of the flat hacl any enmity witl.r the appellants anc'l had no reason to falsely implicate then in this case (ci) That there are no major contradictions in the evidence of tl.re PW's ancl it is rvell settled by now that minor contradictions which clo not effect the materiality of the evidence can be ignorecl ln this resPect reliance is placed on Zakir Khan V State (i995 SCMR 1793)' (e) That we clo not fintl any cleficiencies in the weighing of the narcotics ancl that the correct weight has been recorded as per memo of recovery antl chemical report' (f) That the recovered narcotics were kePt in safe custocly at thc Malkana and were sent for chemical analysis without any clelav ancl such chemical rePort was Positive and compliecl with all relevant legal requirements. (g) 'Ihat is extremely clifficult for such a large amount of heroin to be foisted on the appellants which is usually not readily available with the police/ANF and if the police/ANF had wanted to falsely implicate the appellants they would not have wasted their time making up an ingenious story of the narcotic capsules being hidden in onions and even recovering 1,000 KG of onions, glue and cuttil.tg equipment for the purpose from the flat. They would l.rave simply 7 \ 5 I 26l foistetl tl.re apPellants with a plastic shopper full of narcotics' Instead all the aforesaicl onions, cutters, glue etc were recovered along with the heroin and even 84 preserved onion samples were exhibited at trail where some of the narcotics hatl been hitlden' (h) That since onions are perishable goods most of tlre recovereci 1,000 KG of onions (less the 84 which were exhibited) wete auctioned off and evcrr tlle auction receipt and receipt of Payment was exhibited ancl as such they were undoubtedly in the flat and were recovered along with the narcotics at the time of the raid' (i) That although no inllepenr'lent mashir was associatctl with the arrest and recovery of the appellants it has corne in evidence that the PW's asked inclependent Persons to become lnashers lrut they refused and the police /ANF in any event had to nrove quicklv to ensure that the accusecl did not leave the flat with the narcotics' Even otherra,ise S.103 Cr.PC is excluded for offcnses falling under the Control of Narcotic Substances Act 1997 by virtue of 5 25 of that (j) There is no legal bar for tl-re complainant also being the 10 of tl.re case as ir.t this case 11. Thus, for the reasons mentionecl above, we find that the Prosecution has proved its case beyond a reasonable doubt against the appellants and the impugned judgment is upheld and the appeals are dismissed' 12 The appeals are clisposed on iu the above terms Y tqil ND6d ztf,z)z-,f \)' (r A l
